title: 2025 ELSO Consensus Statement for the Provision and Management of Nutrition Therapy in Critically Ill Adult Patients Requiring Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation
one_line_summary: The ELSO consensus statement provides the first comprehensive, evidence-based guidelines for nutrition therapy in critically ill adult patients requiring ECMO, addressing unique metabolic and circuit-related challenges.
key_pearls: Integrate an ICU Dietitian within 24 hours of ECMO cannulation for specialized nutritional oversight.
Target 25 kcal/kg/day and 1.3 g/kg/day protein, but limit delivery to <70% of targets for the first 3 days of ECMO.
Initiate early enteral nutrition (within 48 hours) if hemodynamically stable; delay or provide trophic feeds (10-20 ml/hr) in uncontrolled shock.
Manage gastrointestinal intolerance with prokinetics (GRV >= 250 ml), postpyloric tubes, or supplemental parenteral nutrition.
Monitor plasma triglycerides twice weekly and visually inspect the oxygenator daily; transition to lipid-free PN if triglycerides >3 mmol/L or lipid layering is observed.
For awake ECMO patients, escalate to enteral nutrition if voluntary oral intake is <50-75% of targets for more than 72 hours.
consensus_method: Systematic review and expert consensus via blind online voting with predefined agreement thresholds (Strong Consensus: 100%, Consensus: 75-99%).

recommendation_blocks.narrative: The guideline strongly recommends that a registered dietitian (or a clinician with specialist clinical nutrition knowledge) be integrated as a key member of the multidisciplinary team (MDT) managing patients receiving ECMO. The metabolic complexity of ECMO patients—characterized by rapid shifts in fluid balance, drug-nutrient interactions, and high risk of gastrointestinal dysfunction—requires specialized, dedicated nutritional oversight. Dietitians are uniquely trained to perform comprehensive nutritional assessments, calculate dynamic energy and protein targets, manage enteral feeding intolerance, and coordinate transitions between EN, PN, and oral intake. In healthcare systems where dedicated ICU dietitians are not part of standard practice, the panel recommends designating an identified nutrition specialist from another profession (e.g., a specialized nutrition nurse or physician) to lead this clinical domain.

recommendations.statement: Integrate a registered dietitian or clinician with specialist clinical nutrition knowledge as a key member of the multidisciplinary team managing ECMO patients.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: The panel recommends that a comprehensive nutrition assessment, including a detailed physical assessment, be performed by a specialist dietitian or clinical nutrition expert within 72 hours of ICU admission and weekly thereafter. Standard screening tools (such as the Subjective Global Assessment [SGA] or the Global Leadership Initiative on Malnutrition [GLIM] criteria) are acceptable. However, clinicians must recognize that these tools can significantly underestimate nutritional risk or malnutrition status upon admission. Factors such as younger age, acute-onset respiratory failure (e.g., severe ARDS), and massive fluid resuscitation can mask underlying muscle wasting and nutritional depletion. Physical assessment should focus on identifying pre-existing muscle wasting, assessing loss of subcutaneous fat, and evaluating fluid status. If validated screening tools are not utilized, clinicians must collectively evaluate pre-ICU length of stay, projected ICU length of stay, and physical assessment findings to risk-stratify the patient. Observational data from Ferrie et al. indicated that over 90% of patients (N = 86) admitted for ECMO were classified as "normally nourished" using SGA, yet they experienced rapid, severe muscle wasting post-admission, highlighting the limitations of baseline screening tools.

recommendations.statement: Perform a comprehensive nutrition assessment, including a detailed physical assessment, by a specialist dietitian or clinical nutrition expert within 72 hours of ICU admission and weekly thereafter.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: The guideline recommends that all patients receiving ECMO support be considered candidates for medical nutrition therapy (MNT), regardless of their pre-existing nutritional status. The initiation of ECMO triggers a profound inflammatory response, and the underlying pathologies (e.g., cardiogenic shock, severe ARDS, sepsis) induce a highly catabolic state. Delaying MNT—even in patients who are well-nourished at baseline—exacerbates skeletal muscle proteolysis, impairs immune function, and increases the risk of infectious complications. This is particularly critical for patients bridged to lung or heart transplantation, where pre-existing chronic organ failure has already depleted nutritional reserves. The decision to initiate MNT must take into account the metabolic consequences of ECLS, the duration of pre-ICU illness, and the projected ICU and hospital length of stay.

recommendations.statement: Consider all patients receiving ECMO support as candidates for medical nutrition therapy (MNT), irrespective of pre-existing nutritional status.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Timing and Dosing of Enteral Nutrition
recommendation_blocks.narrative: The panel recommends that early enteral nutrition (initiated within 48 hours of ECMO initiation) be considered for all patients where EN is not explicitly contraindicated. However, in patients experiencing uncontrolled, severe shock (characterized by escalating doses of vasopressors, worsening lactic acidosis, and severe hemodynamic instability), the panel recommends delaying EN or commencing it at a very low "trophic" dose (e.g., 10 to 20 ml/hour), accompanied by rigorous daily clinical reviews to prevent prolonged underfeeding. This recommendation balances the physiological benefits of early enteral stimulation (maintenance of gut mucosal barrier integrity, reduction of bacterial translocation, and modulation of systemic inflammation) against the catastrophic risk of splanchnic ischemia. Once hemodynamic stability is achieved (stable or de-escalating vasopressor requirements, normalizing lactate), EN should be progressively titrated to target. Retrospective data from Ohbe et al. (N = 1,769 patients on VA-ECMO for cardiogenic or obstructive shock) demonstrated that early EN was associated with significantly lower 28-day mortality and in-hospital mortality compared to late EN.

recommendations.statement: Consider early enteral nutrition (within 48 hours of ECMO initiation) for all patients where EN is not explicitly contraindicated.

Delay enteral nutrition or commence at a trophic dose (e.g., 10-20 ml/hour) in patients with uncontrolled, severe shock (escalating vasopressors, worsening lactic acidosis, severe hemodynamic instability).

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Enteral feeding intolerance (EFI) is highly prevalent during ECMO, driven by splanchnic hypoperfusion, high-dose opioid and sedative infusions, and neuromuscular blockade. The guideline provides a structured, stepwise algorithm for managing EFI: Delayed gastric emptying should be defined using local gastric residual volume (GRV) protocols, but the threshold for intervention should not be set lower than 250 ml measured 4-hourly. For patients with persistent delayed gastric emptying refractory to prokinetic therapy, the panel recommends the placement of a postpyloric (nasojejunal) feeding tube. If postpyloric tube placement is unavailable, logistically unfeasible, or unsuccessful, the initiation of parenteral nutrition (PN) is recommended to avoid severe cumulative nutritional deficits. Regular review of laxative dosing is recommended to maintain bowel motility and prevent ileus, while avoiding excessive stool output (defined as > 1 L/day) which can lead to severe fluid loss, electrolyte depletion, and skin breakdown. The panel explicitly recommends *against* switching to hyperosmolar, energy-dense concentrated enteral formulas solely to reduce fluid volume, as this has been shown to exacerbate gastrointestinal dysfunction and mucosal injury.

recommendations.statement: Use prokinetic agents (metoclopramide or erythromycin) for delayed gastric emptying, defined by gastric residual volume (GRV) >= 250 ml measured 4-hourly.
Place a postpyloric feeding tube for persistent delayed gastric emptying refractory to prokinetics; if unavailable or unsuccessful, initiate parenteral nutrition.
Review laxative dosing regularly to maintain bowel motility and prevent ileus, avoiding stool output > 1 L/day.
Do not switch to hyperosmolar, energy-dense concentrated enteral formulas solely to reduce fluid volume.

recommendation_blocks.topic: Indications and Safety of Parenteral Nutrition
recommendation_blocks.narrative: Parenteral nutrition is a vital therapeutic modality when EN is contraindicated or tolerated poorly. The panel recommends that the patient's pre-ECMO ICU length of stay be factored into the decision-making process. PN (either supplemental or total) should be initiated within 72 hours of documented EN intolerance or when enteral access cannot be safely established. A critical safety concern unique to ECMO is the administration of intravenous lipid emulsions (ILE) via PN. Lipids can interact with the polyurethane or polymethylpentene fibers of the membrane oxygenator, leading to lipid "layering," visual clotting, and premature circuit failure. To mitigate this risk, the panel recommends twice-weekly monitoring of plasma triglyceride levels and daily visual inspection of the membrane oxygenator for signs of lipid deposition or clotting. If plasma triglyceride levels exceed 3 mmol/L (approximately 265 mg/dL), or if there are visual signs of circuit clotting, clinicians should consider transitioning to a lipid-free PN formulation. To prevent essential fatty acid deficiency (EFAD) during prolonged lipid-free PN, clinicians can administer weekly lipid infusions (under close monitoring) and daily fat-soluble vitamins.

recommendations.statement: Initiate parenteral nutrition (PN) within 72 hours of documented enteral nutrition (EN) intolerance or when enteral access cannot be safely established.
Monitor plasma triglyceride levels twice-weekly and visually inspect the membrane oxygenator daily for lipid deposition or clotting.
Consider transitioning to a lipid-free PN formulation if plasma triglycerides exceed 3 mmol/L (265 mg/dL) or if visual signs of circuit clotting are present.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Determining energy expenditure in ECMO patients is highly challenging. While indirect calorimetry (IC) is the gold standard, standard IC devices cannot account for the carbon dioxide removed and oxygen added by the membrane oxygenator. Although specialized, multi-device IC protocols have been described in the literature, they are not widely available or validated for routine clinical use. Therefore, in the absence of a validated, institutional IC protocol, the panel recommends using a simple, weight-based predictive formula of **25 kcal/kg of body weight/day** to estimate baseline energy targets. Regarding titration, the panel recommends a hypocaloric feeding strategy during the hyperacute phase of illness. Clinicians should aim for **< 70% of estimated or measured energy targets for the first 3 days of ECMO**, followed by a progressive, stepwise increase to 100% of target over the subsequent days, while strictly avoiding prolonged underfeeding. This strategy minimizes the risk of overfeeding-induced metabolic complications (such as hyperglycemia, hypercapnia, and hepatic steatosis) during the phase of mitochondrial dysfunction and endogenous energy production.

recommendations.statement: Use a weight-based predictive formula of 25 kcal/kg of body weight/day to estimate baseline energy targets in the absence of a validated indirect calorimetry protocol.
Aim for < 70% of estimated or measured energy targets for the first 3 days of ECMO, then progressively increase to 100%.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: Protein delivery is critical to attenuate skeletal muscle wasting, but excessive protein loading during the acute phase of critical illness may be harmful, particularly in patients with acute kidney injury (AKI) or high severity of illness. Based on recent clinical trial data (such as the EFFORT Protein Trial), the panel recommends aiming for a target protein dose of **no more than 1.3 g/kg of body weight/day** during the acute phase of illness. Similar to energy titration, the panel recommends a restricted protein strategy during the initial phase of ECMO support: aiming for **< 70% of the protein target for the first 3 days**, followed by a progressive increase to the full target of 1.3 g/kg/day as hemodynamic stability is established. This hypoproteic initial phase avoids overloading the urea cycle and worsening metabolic acidosis during the peak of systemic shock.

recommendations.statement: Aim for a target protein dose of no more than 1.3 g/kg of body weight/day during the acute phase of illness.
Aim for < 70% of the protein target for the first 3 days of ECMO, then progressively increase to the full target of 1.3 g/kg/day.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: The panel recommends that the monitoring of medical nutrition therapy be conducted under the direct guidance of a critical care dietitian or nutrition specialist. Monitoring must be comprehensive and dynamic, encompassing: Daily tracking of cumulative energy and protein balances. Close monitoring of serum phosphate, potassium, and magnesium levels upon initiation of feeding, with aggressive electrolyte replacement as needed. Continuous monitoring of blood glucose levels (targeting a range of 140 to 180 mg/dL) and insulin requirements. Serial physical examinations of the abdomen, monitoring of stool frequency and consistency, and measurement of GRVs (using a standardized, non-restrictive protocol).

recommendations.statement: Conduct comprehensive and dynamic monitoring of medical nutrition therapy under the direct guidance of a critical care dietitian or nutrition specialist.

recommendation_blocks.narrative: A growing clinical trend in ECLS is the "awake ECMO" strategy, where patients (particularly those bridged to lung transplantation) are extubated, mobilized, and kept awake while on support. While this strategy avoids the complications of mechanical ventilation and sedation, it presents unique nutritional challenges. Awake patients frequently experience severe anorexia, dyspnea, taste alterations, and dry oral mucosa (exacerbated by high-flow nasal oxygen), leading to poor oral intake. Additionally, the presence of a nasogastric tube can cause significant discomfort and impair swallowing. The panel recommends that if an awake ECMO patient's voluntary oral intake remains **between 50% and 75% of their calculated energy and protein targets for more than 72 hours**, despite maximal oral nutrition support strategies (such as high-protein oral nutritional supplements, food fortification, and dietetic counseling), clinicians should strongly consider escalating to enteral nutrition (via a small-bore feeding tube) to prevent rapid nutritional and functional decline.

recommendations.statement: If an awake ECMO patient's voluntary oral intake is <50-75% of calculated targets for >72 hours, strongly consider escalating to enteral nutrition via a small-bore feeding tube.

bedside_protocol.title: Phase 1: Admission & Baseline Assessment (Within 72 Hours of ECMO Initiation)
bedside_protocol.action: Automatically consult the ICU Dietitian within 24 hours of ECMO cannulation. Perform a physical assessment for baseline muscle wasting (temporalis, clavicular, and interosseous wasting). Document pre-ICU length of stay and calculate dry body weight (or use documented dry weight prior to fluid resuscitation). Screen for refeeding syndrome risk (history of chronic alcohol abuse, severe weight loss, or prolonged starvation). Establish baseline targets: Energy at 25 kcal/kg of dry body weight/day and Protein at 1.3 g/kg of dry body weight/day.

bedside_protocol.title: Phase 2: Initiation & Hypocaloric Titration (Days 1 to 3 of ECMO)
bedside_protocol.action: Hemodynamic Gatekeeper: If the patient has escalating vasopressor requirements (e.g., Norepinephrine > 0.2 micrograms/kg/min), worsening lactic acidosis (Lactate > 3.0 mmol/L), or severe mean arterial pressure (MAP) instability, delay EN or initiate at a trophic rate of 10 ml/hour of a standard polymeric formula. If vasopressor doses are stable or de-escalating, lactate is < 2.0 mmol/L, and perfusion is adequate, initiate EN within 48 hours via a nasogastric tube at 20 ml/hour. Dosing Target (Days 1-3): Limit delivery to < 70% of calculated targets (Max 17.5 kcal/kg/day energy and Max 0.9 g/kg/day protein).

bedside_protocol.title: Phase 3: Stepwise Escalation & Maintenance (Day 4+ of ECMO)
bedside_protocol.action: Titration Rule: If the patient tolerates EN without signs of gastrointestinal intolerance, increase the infusion rate by 20 ml/hour every 12 to 24 hours to reach 100% of calculated targets (25 kcal/kg/day and 1.3 g/kg/day) by Day 5 to 7. Gastrointestinal Intolerance Protocol (If EFI occurs): Definition of EFI: Gastric Residual Volume (GRV) >= 250 ml on two consecutive 4-hourly measurements, or acute abdominal distension. Step 1 (Prokinetics): Initiate Metoclopramide 10 mg IV every 8 hours AND/OR Erythromycin 100 mg to 250 mg IV every 8 hours. Step 2 (Postpyloric Tube): If GRV remains >= 250 ml despite 24 hours of dual prokinetic therapy, place a postpyloric feeding tube (using electromagnetic or endoscopic guidance). Step 3 (Supplemental PN): If postpyloric placement fails or is unavailable, and enteral delivery remains < 60% of target for > 72 hours, initiate Supplemental Parenteral Nutrition (SPN). Laxative Rule: Administer daily laxatives to maintain bowel movements. If stool output exceeds 1 L/day, reduce or hold laxatives and check serum electrolytes (potassium, magnesium, phosphate) every 12 hours.

bedside_protocol.title: Phase 4: Parenteral Nutrition & Circuit Safety Monitoring
bedside_protocol.action: Triglyceride Monitoring: Draw serum triglyceride levels prior to PN initiation, then twice weekly thereafter. Visual Circuit Inspection: The bedside nurse must inspect the membrane oxygenator every 12 hours (at shift change) for visual signs of lipid layering (milky or white discoloration in the header or fibers) or micro-thrombi. Lipid-Free PN Trigger: If serum Triglycerides are > 3.0 mmol/L, OR if visual lipid layering or rapid clot accumulation is noted in the oxygenator: Immediately transition to a lipid-free PN formulation. Administer water-soluble and fat-soluble vitamins daily. To prevent EFAD during prolonged support, administer a single weekly infusion of a mixed-lipid emulsion over 12 to 24 hours under continuous visual and biochemical monitoring.

bedside_protocol.title: Phase 5: Transition & "Awake" ECMO Protocol
bedside_protocol.action: Oral Intake Tracking: For extubated, awake patients on ECMO, document all oral intake using a 24-hour food diary. Enteral Escalation Trigger: If voluntary oral intake is < 50% to 75% of calculated targets for > 72 hours: Place a small-bore nasogastric feeding tube and initiate supplemental EN (nocturnal or continuous low-dose) to bridge the nutritional gap and prevent functional decline.

strengths_limitations: Strengths include a rigorous, predefined systematic review methodology, a multidisciplinary international expert panel from high-volume ECLS centers, and clear grading of consensus with explicit percentage thresholds. Limitations are primarily the complete absence of randomized controlled trial data, meaning all recommendations are based on observational studies or extrapolation. Further limitations include the heterogeneity of ECMO populations (VV vs. VA ECMO) grouped together for many recommendations, and the lack of commercially available, validated indirect calorimetry protocols that integrate both ventilator and membrane oxygenator gas exchange.
